name: No 89
sentiment: Floris's most celebrated masculine — a proper post-war British fougère with genuine oakmoss and a carnation heart that few houses attempt anymore.
similar: Boucheron Pour Homme, Creed Green Irish Tweed, Czech & Speake Oxford & Cambridge
unique: The carnation-rose heart is unusual in a fougère — it gives No 89 a spicy warmth that the pure lavender-geranium route doesn't achieve.
collector: A benchmark for traditional British masculine perfumery. Floris has supplied the British Royal Family since the 18th century.
source: Notes: Floris London press disclosures. Performance: community aggregation (Fragrantica, Basenotes).

name: Elite
sentiment: A lighter, more accessible companion to No 89 — cleaner and less oakmoss-heavy but still recognisably Floris in character.
similar: Floris No 89, Penhaligon's Blenheim Bouquet, Truefitt and Hill Grafton
unique: Lily of the Valley in the heart adds a delicate freshness that bridges the neroli top and oakmoss base gracefully.
beginner: More approachable entry to Floris's masculine range than No 89.
source: Notes: Floris London press disclosures. Performance: community aggregation (Fragrantica).

name: Cefiro
sentiment: Floris's Mediterranean riff — a cheerful citrus with a lavender-rosemary midpoint. Better longevity than most traditional colognes.
similar: Truefitt and Hill Apsley, Czech & Speake Oxford & Cambridge, Penhaligon's Blenheim Bouquet
unique: Petitgrain alongside grapefruit and bergamot gives the opening a leafy, green dimension that's more interesting than a pure squeeze.
beginner: The most approachable Floris masculine — a light, agreeable citrus that wears easily.
source: Notes: Floris London press disclosures. Performance: community aggregation (Fragrantica).

name: Santal
sentiment: One of the finest sandalwood-led masculines in the Floris range — warm, spiced, and with a real feel of the orient filtered through an English lens.
similar: Chanel Égoïste, Guerlain Habit Rouge, Penhaligon's Sartorial
unique: Benzoin in the base deepens the sandalwood into something more resinous and rounded than a pure Mysore construction.
collector: The 1975 sandalwood origin is genuinely high-quality — a benchmark for the genre at the designer tier.
source: Notes: Floris London press disclosures. Performance: community aggregation (Fragrantica, Basenotes).

name: Bouquet de la Reine
sentiment: A Victorian floral chypre revived — formal, powdery, and completely foreign to modern noses. Wear it as a history lesson.
similar: Penhaligon's Quercus, Grossmith Shem-el-Nessim, Guerlain Mitsouko
unique: Civet base gives this pre-reformulation floral its original animalic character — a texture the modern version has moderated.
collector: Of interest for its 1901 heritage and the quality of Floris's recreation.
source: Notes: Floris London press disclosures. Performance: community aggregation (Fragrantica, Basenotes).

name: Oxford & Cambridge
sentiment: Czech & Speake's founding fragrance — a textbook English fougère that has been refined rather than changed over four decades.
similar: Floris No 89, Penhaligon's Blenheim Bouquet, Truefitt and Hill 1805
unique: Tonka Bean in the base adds a gentle almond-coumarin warmth that shifts it from austere chypre territory into something more approachable.
beginner: An excellent introduction to the Jermyn Street barbershop fougère tradition.
source: Notes: Czech & Speake press disclosures. Performance: community aggregation (Fragrantica, Basenotes).

name: No 88
sentiment: Czech & Speake's best — an earthy, vetiver-forward fougère that's denser and more masculine than Oxford & Cambridge.
similar: Guerlain Vetiver, Creed Original Vetiver, Floris No 89
unique: Lime in the opening alongside bergamot gives a sharper, more tart start than the classic bergamot-only fougère — bracing and distinctive.
collector: Consistently regarded as one of the finest traditional British fougères in continuous production.
source: Notes: Czech & Speake press disclosures. Performance: community aggregation (Fragrantica, Basenotes).

name: Dark Rose
sentiment: Czech & Speake pushing into darker territory — tobacco and leather swallow the rose into something genuinely brooding.
similar: Tom Ford Beauty Noir de Noir, Serge Lutens Sa Majesté la Rose, Juliette Has a Gun Lady Vengeance
unique: The rose is present but peripheral — tobacco and leather dominate in a way that makes this a rose oriental rather than a floral.
source: Notes: Czech & Speake press disclosures. Performance: community aggregation (Fragrantica).

name: Cubana
sentiment: A rum-soaked tobacco aromatic inspired by Havana — rich, slightly sweet, and genuinely evocative. Czech & Speake at its most adventurous.
similar: Tom Ford Beauty Tobacco Vanille, Maison Margiela Replica Jazz Club, Serge Lutens Chergui
unique: Rum note in the opening reads as actual liquor, not synthetic sweetness — it evaporates leaving dry tobacco with a lasting warmth.
collector: Among the most character-driven offerings in the Czech & Speake range.
source: Notes: Czech & Speake press disclosures. Performance: community aggregation (Fragrantica).

name: Extract of Limes
sentiment: One of the oldest continuously produced colognes in London — sharp, authentic lime rather than the synthetic twist of most modern citrus fragrances.
similar: Floris Cefiro, Truefitt and Hill Apsley, Penhaligon's Blenheim Bouquet
unique: Genuine extract rather than synthetic lime aroma — the brief, tart authenticity is the point.
beginner: A classic post-shave or morning cologne. Short-lived but the quality is real.
collector: Carries historical significance as one of the original Jermyn Street barber fragrances.
source: Notes: Geo F. Trumper press disclosures. Performance: community aggregation (Fragrantica, Basenotes).

name: Eucris
sentiment: A pre-WWI herbal fougère that remains in production — medicinal eucalyptus gives it a fresh edge unlike anything in contemporary masculine perfumery.
similar: Floris No 89, Czech & Speake Oxford & Cambridge, Penhaligon's English Fern
unique: Eucalyptus opens like a hospital corridor, then eucalyptus and caraway together settle into something unexpectedly appealing.
collector: One of the few continuously produced pre-WWI barbershop fragrances. Historical significance beyond the juice.
source: Notes: Geo F. Trumper press disclosures. Performance: community aggregation (Fragrantica, Basenotes).

name: Ajaccio Violets
sentiment: A Victorian violet cologne still made exactly as originally intended — powdery, gentle, and deliberately old-fashioned.
similar: Penhaligon's Violetta, Czech & Speake Oxford & Cambridge, Floris Elite
unique: Orris in the heart turns the violet from purely floral to something rooty and powdery — the Victorian masculine interpretation of the note.
collector: Historically interesting as a surviving example of Victorian barbershop violet tradition.
source: Notes: Geo F. Trumper press disclosures. Performance: community aggregation (Fragrantica).

name: Sandalwood
sentiment: A straightforward sandalwood barber cologne — not complex, but made with quality materials and an honest unpretentious character.
similar: Floris Santal, Penhaligon's English Fern, Czech & Speake No 88
unique: The sandalwood is the star at every stage — this was built before layering complexity became a design goal.
beginner: Unchallenging, pleasant, and long-wearing for the genre.
source: Notes: Geo F. Trumper press disclosures. Performance: community aggregation (Fragrantica).

name: Grafton
sentiment: The flagship of the world's oldest barbershop — a well-made fougère that embodies Jermyn Street quiet confidence.
similar: Czech & Speake Oxford & Cambridge, Floris No 89, Penhaligon's Blenheim Bouquet
unique: Tonka bean in the base adds just enough sweetness to lift the oakmoss into something approachable rather than austere.
beginner: A gentle entry into traditional British barbershop fragrance.
collector: Truefitt and Hill (est. 1805) is the world's oldest barbershop — the Grafton carries that heritage.
source: Notes: Truefitt and Hill press disclosures. Performance: community aggregation (Fragrantica, Basenotes).

name: Wellington
sentiment: Darker and more commanding than Grafton — the leather note gives it a military-meets-drawing-room gravitas.
similar: Floris Santal, Dunhill for Men, Creed Bois du Portugal
unique: Rosemary-leather midpoint is more structured than the typical lavender-sandalwood route — precise and slightly formal.
source: Notes: Truefitt and Hill press disclosures. Performance: community aggregation (Fragrantica).

sentiment: The eponymous founding-year fragrance — a simple, honest herbal fougère that doesn't overclaim. Better as a daily-use barber water than an evening statement.
similar: Truefitt and Hill Grafton, Floris Cefiro, Czech & Speake Oxford & Cambridge
unique: The most rosemary-forward in the T&H range — a bracing, almost medicinal freshness before the sandalwood calms it.
beginner: Pleasant, easy-wearing, reasonably priced for the heritage.
source: Notes: Truefitt and Hill press disclosures. Performance: community aggregation (Fragrantica).

name: Apsley
sentiment: Truefitt's modern face — a clean, citrus-lavender fresh built for contemporary wearers rather than the barbershop faithful.
similar: Czech & Speake Oxford & Cambridge, Floris Cefiro, Hugo Boss Bottled
unique: Grapefruit-bergamot opening is more modern and bright than the lemon-dominant 1805 — a conscious update for the house.
beginner: The most contemporary and approachable fragrance in the Truefitt range.
source: Notes: Truefitt and Hill press disclosures. Performance: community aggregation (Fragrantica).

name: Re-charge Black Pepper
sentiment: Molton Brown's bestselling masculine — a no-nonsense pepper aromatic that does exactly what it says. Reliable and well-made.
similar: Penhaligon's Sartorial, Czech & Speake No 88, Comme des Garçons Pepper
unique: Coriander alongside cardamom in the top gives this more citrus-spice warmth than a pure peppercorn construction.
beginner: A very approachable, daily-wear spicy aromatic — excellent value for Molton Brown quality.
source: Notes: Molton Brown press disclosures. Performance: community aggregation (Fragrantica).

name: Bushukan
sentiment: Yuzu as the lead ingredient — bright, tart, and distinctly Japanese in character. The best warm-weather Molton Brown masculine.
similar: Hermès Un Jardin sur le Nil, Issey Miyake L'Eau d'Issey, Bulgari Aqua
unique: Japanese Knotweed alongside yuzu is genuinely unusual — adds a slightly bitter, vegetal note that keeps the citrus from being saccharine.
beginner: The most interesting of the Molton Brown freshies and a natural replacement for standard summer colognes.
source: Notes: Molton Brown press disclosures. Performance: community aggregation (Fragrantica).

name: Orange & Bergamot
sentiment: Clean, bright, and predictable — a reliable warm-weather citrus that requires no explanation.
similar: Acqua di Giò, Jo Malone London Lime Basil & Mandarin, L'Occitane Eau des Baux
unique: Ginger in the heart adds just enough warmth to keep the citrus from going watery.
beginner: Easy pick for summer — fresh, clean, unoffensive.
source: Notes: Molton Brown press disclosures. Performance: community aggregation (Fragrantica).

name: Black Peppercorn
sentiment: A dryer, more herbal companion to Re-charge Black Pepper — thyme and rosemary push it into culinary-aromatic territory.
similar: Molton Brown Re-charge Black Pepper, Hermès Eau d'Orange Verte, Penhaligon's Sartorial
unique: Thyme in the heart is underused in masculine fragrances — it gives this a slightly sharp, green-Mediterranean quality.
beginner: More interesting than the average pepper aromatic at the designer price point.
source: Notes: Molton Brown press disclosures. Performance: community aggregation (Fragrantica).

name: L'Air de Rien
sentiment: Created with Jane Birkin — a warm, skin-like musky woody that smells like someone not a bottle. Casually animalic and deeply personal.
similar: Serge Lutens L'Eau, Comme des Garçons 2, Maison Margiela Replica Whispers in the Library
unique: Civet in the base is subtle enough to add warmth rather than offend — a skin-musk effect that improves over hours of wear.
collector: Jane Birkin collaboration with perfumer Lyn Harris — a genuine creative statement rather than a celebrity cash-in.
source: Notes: Miller Harris press disclosures. Performance: community aggregation (Fragrantica, Basenotes).

name: Tom
sentiment: A clean, characterful aromatic fougère — grapefruit gives it a brightness the standard bergamot fougère doesn't have.
similar: Floris Elite, Czech & Speake Oxford & Cambridge, Jo Malone London Wood Sage & Sea Salt
unique: Pepper alongside grapefruit in the top creates a citrus-spice hybrid opening that reads as more modern than the 2003 date suggests.
beginner: A solid, wearable, clearly structured fougère at the Miller Harris standard.
source: Notes: Miller Harris press disclosures. Performance: community aggregation (Fragrantica).

name: Foin Fraîchement Coupé
sentiment: Freshly cut hay and grass rendered with botanical precision — earthy-green and genuinely evocative of a summer meadow.
similar: Creed Green Irish Tweed, Issey Miyake L'Eau d'Issey, Hermès Un Jardin sur le Toit
unique: Hay note is handled naturalistically rather than synthetically — this actually smells like the thing it claims to be.
collector: Lyn Harris's most distinctive masculine — the green hay accord is unusually well-executed for the genre.
source: Notes: Miller Harris press disclosures. Performance: community aggregation (Fragrantica, Basenotes).

name: Shem-el-Nessim
sentiment: A 1908 Edwardian oriental revived with historical accuracy — opulent, aldehydic, and entirely unlike what any modern house would launch.
similar: Houbigant Fougère Royale, Guerlain Jicky, Penhaligon's Quercus
unique: Civet base gives it the animalic warmth of pre-IFRA perfumery — this wears as it was made to be worn, for a drawing room not a conference call.
collector: Grossmith (est. 1835) revived their Victorian catalog. Shem-el-Nessim is the most cited of the revivals.
source: Notes: Grossmith London press disclosures. Performance: community aggregation (Fragrantica, Basenotes).

name: Beau Monde
sentiment: Edwardian floral chypre with oakmoss and labdanum — a composition class you'll never see from a contemporary house.
similar: Guerlain Mitsouko, Robert Piguet Parfums Bandit, Floris Bouquet de la Reine
unique: Labdanum-oakmoss base is the defining element — resiny, mossy, and distinctly pre-modern in the most interesting sense.
collector: Essential for collectors interested in the Edwardian chypre tradition.
source: Notes: Grossmith London press disclosures. Performance: community aggregation (Fragrantica).

name: Hasu-no-Hana
sentiment: A late-Victorian lotus oriental that predates the genre it seems to belong to — delicate, luminous, and unexpectedly modern in feeling.
similar: Floris Bouquet de la Reine, Penhaligon's Violetta, Czech & Speake Dark Rose
unique: Lotus was an exotic rarity in 1888 perfumery — its translucent floral quality gives this a watery lightness that was genuinely innovative.
collector: Victorian lotus fragrance with provenance — one of the original Grossmith compositions now revived.
source: Notes: Grossmith London press disclosures. Performance: community aggregation (Fragrantica).

name: Fougère Royale
sentiment: The fragrance that invented the fougère family in 1882 — Paul Parquet's coumarin-oakmoss accord established the template all subsequent fougères follow.
similar: Czech & Speake No 88, Floris No 89, Azzaro Pour Homme
unique: Coumarin was a synthetic novelty in 1882 — this is the original use of it in perfumery, the founding document of an entire genre.
collector: Every collector of classic masculine perfumery should encounter this — the fougère origin and its modern revival.
source: Notes: Houbigant press disclosures. Historical context: Luca Turin & Tania Sanchez, Perfumes: The Guide. Performance: community aggregation (Fragrantica, Basenotes).

name: Quelques Fleurs Homme
sentiment: A masculine companion to the legendary Quelques Fleurs feminine — floral and aromatic in equal measure, with the Houbigant house quality evident throughout.
similar: Floris Elite, Boucheron Pour Homme, Chanel Pour Monsieur
unique: Carnation alongside jasmine and rose creates a spice-floral combination that resists the usual masculine-feminine binary.
source: Notes: Houbigant press disclosures. Performance: community aggregation (Fragrantica).

name: Duc de Hollande
sentiment: Originally created for King William of Holland — a powdery, formal oriental revival from Houbigant's archive.
similar: Guerlain Jicky, Grossmith Shem-el-Nessim, Floris Bouquet de la Reine
unique: Civet base gives it the animalic character of 19th-century court perfumery — a historical artifact as much as a fragrance.
collector: Royal provenance from 1819, revived by Houbigant from their original archive formulas.
source: Notes: Houbigant press disclosures. Performance: community aggregation (Fragrantica).
